parties: Between Her Majesty the Queen, respondent, and Lorne McConnell, accused (appellant), and Peter Letendre, accused (appellant), and Russell Haffter, accused (not party to appeal)
court: Alberta Court of Appeal Calgary Criminal Sittings Conrad, Côté and Russell JJ.A.
body_html:
Heard: December 5, 1994.
Judgment: filed July 5, 1995. (42 pp.)

On appeal from Sulatycky J.

Statutes, Regulations and Rules Cited:

Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 34(1), 34(2), 231(1), 232, 265(1)(b).

Criminal law -- Offences against person and reputation -- Manslaughter -- Murder -- Self-defence -- When applicable.

These were appeals by two accused, one from his conviction for manslaughter and the other from his conviction for second degree murder. They had been charged jointly with another accused with first degree murder of a fellow penitentiary inmate. The other accused had been acquitted.

HELD: The appeals were dismissed. Self-defence did not apply in the circumstances. Section 34(2) could only be a defence if the accused had a reasonable apprehension of death or grievous bodily harm. Apprehension in general or previous threats did not constitute grounds for application of section 34(2) of the Criminal Code. The accused's evidence with respect to self- defence had no air in reality and the supposed flaws in the jury charge respecting the defence were academic.

Counsel:

B.D. Rosborough, for the respondent.
C.R. Darwent, for the accused (appellant).
A.C. Rice, for the accused (not parties to appeal).

MEMORANDUM OF JUDGMENT

Reasons for judgment were delivered by Côté J.A., concurred in by Russell J.A. Separate reasons were delivered by Conrad J.A.

CÔTÉ J.A. (orally):-- The two appellants and a third man were jointly charged with first degree murder of a fellow penitentiary inmate. The third man was acquitted, the appellant McConnell was convicted of manslaughter, and the appellant Letendre of second degree murder. The facts are fully set out in the factums and Appeal Book, so a brief summary suffices here.

McConnell had had a quarrel with a group of other inmates. The victim was associated with that group, and had insulted and briefly assaulted a friend and emissary of McConnell's. A fight was threatened. Some hours later, the three accused, friends, were in a virtually impregnable position at the door of the cell of one of them. Those who had earlier threatened saw that and made no attempt to approach or attack the three accused. Later still, McConnell saw the victim walking away. McConnell ran up behind, and knocked down the victim, and struck him more than once. Letendre then stabbed the victim in the stomach, likely with a home-made knife. The stab wounds were fatal.

McConnell testified that he thought that the other inmate group were going to kill him, and so he decided to strike first without waiting longer. Letendre did not testify. There was no evidence of any threat to Letendre's safety.

The accused elected trial by jury. The trial judge charged the jury on a number of topics, including self-defence.

On appeal, the factum of the two accused argued that the verdicts were unreasonable, but counsel properly did not pursue that orally. Instead they argued a number of aspects of self-defence: the objective element, the duty to retreat, and immediacy of danger. They alleged flaws in the charge to the jury in those respects.

After judgment was reserved, the Court of Appeal called for and got additional written argument about whether the victim assaulted either of the appellants, and the Court permitted and got argument on defence of third persons.

Clearly Letendre could not hope to succeed on the defence of self-defence. He did not testify, there is very little evidence of any danger to him, and little or no other evidence about Letendre's thoughts or mental state at or before the time he stabbed the victim. Self-defence should not have been left to the jury in his case.

McConnell knocked the victim down with knuckledusters, and repeatedly beat him when he was down. The injuries to the victim were fairly serious. The pathologist used the word "superficial" only to mean that the skull was not fractured, but he said that they were fairly good blows. He also testified that unconsciousness and concussion may well have resulted, and that there was a host of wounds to the skin of the head and face, including a number of open gaping tears, and bruising, all from the blunt weapon used.

Self-defence is codified in Canada. Section 34(1) of the Criminal Code applies only if the force used is not intended to cause death or grievous bodily harm. McConnell's blows did cause grievous bodily harm to the head, and were plainly intended to. And McConnell testified that he intended to kill the victim. So s. 34(1) gives no defence.

Indeed, the appellants' joint factum says that s. 34(2) was argued at trial. Section 34(2) gives a defence of self-defence only to one who is unlawfully assaulted and causes the death "in repelling the assault". Threats had been made earlier in the day and had reached McConnell indirectly, but he was not present when they were made. We cannot see how they could be assaults. One can assault only if one is close enough to carry out one's threats. See Criminal Code s. 265(1)(b) on present ability to effect the purpose. That need for present ability to effect the purpose of the one threatening was correctly put to the jury, and no one has ever complained of that part of the charge. Later, the victim came to the general neighbourhood of where McConnell and his cell were, but did not come close enough to do any harm, nor did he then threaten any, and he was going away. So that could not be an assault. There never was an assault against the accused, nor by the victim, nor by the victim's friends.

While the victim was walking away, McConnell ran after him and knocked the victim down with the knuckledusters. We do not know how one can say that that was "repelling the assault". First, there never was an assault on McConnell. Second, even if there had been one, it was over then. Third, we do not see how running up behind a person who is walking away and knocking him or her down, can be called repelling his or her assault.

Furthermore, s. 34(2) gives a defence only if the accused had a reasonable apprehension of his own death or (grievous bodily harm). The subsection says that that apprehension cannot merely arise in general, nor from previous threats, but must come from one of two things. The first is "from the violence with which the assault was originally made"; that plainly cannot apply here. The second is [from the violence] "with which the assailant pursues his purposes". There was no violence at all by the victim or his friends. So we do not see how one even gets to s. 34(2)(b). The defence falls down before then.

Therefore, the defence of self-defence had no air of reality. It should not have been left to the jury, and the jury properly rejected it. Therefore, the supposed flaws in parts of the jury charge about self-defence which were first argued on this appeal become academic.

The defence of defending others arose at a late stage. Part of the Crown's comment is as follows:

"These defences were not raised at trial by any of 3 competent counsel. There was no objection to the charge to the jury on the basis of the absence of any of these. Their absence was not a ground of appeal. These arguments were not raised in the factum of the Appellants. These arguments were not raised at the hearing of this appeal."

Section 27 of the Criminal Code allows the use of as much force as is reasonably necessary to prevent commission of a serious offence likely to cause immediate and serious injury. Beating and stabbing a person who was leaving and now lies injured on the ground scarcely so qualifies, especially when there are armed peace officers nearby. Section 37 allows as much force as is necessary (but no more) to defend a person under one's protection from assault. All three elements of that are lacking here: reasonable force, assault, and protection.

The appeals are dismissed.

CÔTÉ J.A.
RUSSELL J.A.

CONRAD J.A. (dissenting):-- I have had the opportunity of reviewing the judgment of Côté J.A. and, with respect, am unable to agree with his conclusion. The trial judge felt there was an air of reality to the defence of self-defence and elected to leave it with the jury. While the evidence with respect to that defence has weaknesses, particularly in the case of Letendre, I cannot say it is wrong. The main issue becomes whether or not there was an error in the charge to the jury with respect to the defence of self-defence.

The appellants filed a joint factum in which they argue that the trial judge misdirected the jury on self-defence in three material ways: the objective element of self-defence, the duty to retreat, and the requisite immediacy of the danger. Because the defence is dependent upon the unique fact situation of the case, I propose to set out the facts in some detail.

Background Facts

The appellants and the deceased, Leslie Allan Casey, were prisoners in the Drumheller Penitentiary when Casey was stabbed to death in Unit 10 of the prison. Prior to the incident there had been considerable tension between McConnell and the Inmates Committee relating to the operation of the canteen. The Inmates Committee is a powerful body in the prison environment and exerts considerable influence over the constitution of the Canteen Committee.

The Inmates Committee consisted of the prisoners Marrigan, Pratt, Childs and Kreba. McConnell had had differences with Marrigan with respect to the operation of the Canteen Committee. Committee members would use income generated by the canteen to earn money for themselves either by loansharking the currency to other inmates, requiring repayment plus 50 per cent interest on the next day, or giving credit to inmates through the canteen, requiring repayment of the credit amount, plus 20 per cent interest, at the next pay period. McConnell disliked these methods of earning income, feeling that it was theft. He wanted to be on the Canteen Committee to curb its loansharking.

Casey was a member of the Canteen Committee; he was the person who kept the books for the canteen and would collect outstanding debts. In essence, he was the canteen's "heavy" or "enforcer". McConnell had never had a direct conflict with Casey before the day that Casey was killed.

Torn Down Poster - Tension Building

Earlier on the day of the crime, a confrontation had occurred between McConnell and Pratt and Marrigan as a result of the removal of certain posters which McConnell had placed in the gym. McConnell had placed a poster in the gym requesting participants to register for a weightlifting competition. That poster had been torn down. He then posted a new sign which stated that some "spineless individual" had torn down the previous sign. He was advised that Childs and Marrigan had torn down the poster, because they thought it was an eyesore, they didn't like it and questioned his authority to hold a competition without the committee's approval. McConnell asked Marrigan why the poster had been torn down, and Marrigan told him that they didn't like the poster, they didn't like him naming the competition without consulting them, and they didn't like him. McConnell responded by telling him that was fine, that they should each stay away from each other, and they would do good time in the prison.

The Threats

At approximately 3:30 that afternoon, Marrigan sent a message that he had wanted to see McConnell. McConnell armed himself with knuckle-dusters and went to see Marrigan in Pratt's cell, where he found both Pratt and Marrigan. Marrigan was intoxicated by drugs and started asking McConnell what authority he had to do anything without consulting the committee. McConnell's evidence was that he thought he was "all pilled up" so he just left and said, "Well, whatever, John. You know whatever is whatever." And then he turned and walked out.

As he left, Pratt came tearing out of the cell, apparently wanting to fight. McConnell's reaction was to reach back for his weapon and say "Let's get at her." At this point another inmate, Steven Czupor, was preparing to hand Pratt a shank (home-made knife). Pratt seemed to have second thoughts and backed off. McConnell then heard Marrigan say: "We'll take care of this after supper. I got a lot of friends around here. We'll see how -- we'll see how fucking tough you are after supper." McConnell responded by saying he would have to drain every drop of blood out of him before he fell.

Following the confrontation, McConnell testified that he believed Marrigan would put together a group of his friends to stab him or beat him up, and that he took the threat seriously. From his cell window, which faced into the courtyard, he could see Pratt, Marrigan, Childs and Czupor running around talking to people, after which the people would take off. He was of the impression they were going to obtain weapons.

¶ 26 McConnell did not go for supper that evening but stayed on his Range. He sent a friend, Wayne Carson, to see Marrigan to propose a truce. Carlson returned about five minutes later to inform McConnell that Marrigan had said things had gone too far for a truce.

Another inmate and friend of McConnell's, Kevin May, a one-armed man, also attempted to ease the confrontation that was developing. He asked Pratt and Marrigan to act as a go-between, with the objective of cooling down the dispute between them and McConnell. He was struck by Casey and told to get out because if he stayed in the middle he would die in the middle.

At the preliminary inquiry, the Crown called Czupor, who testified that matters had gone too far and that "somebody had to go". That evidence was read in at discovery and confirmed the seriousness of the attack that McConnell was anticipating.

Czupor testified as to the hostility between the Inmates Committee and McConnell. He described McConnell as a loner and a man who stood his own ground. He also said that when one is alone in prison, and threats have been made, one either gets locked up or knocked off. About the dispute between McConnell and the Committee, Czupor said:

Q. Had you heard any of the conversation that happened just before that?
A. Kevin May wanted to cool the beef out.
Q. I am sorry.
A. Kevin May wanted to cool the beef out, he was pleading for the beef to end.
Q. By beef what do you mean?
A. Well, events so far is somebody had to go.
Q. Okay. They were having an argument, discussion?
A. That is mildly putting it.
(A.B. Vol. IV, p. 590) (emphasis added)

He also confirmed that prisoners do "beef up" when anticipating an attack and place things under their clothes for protection. This is not done when prisoners are going out to attack someone. Czupor also testified about the loansharking and drug trafficking activities of the Inmates Committee in the prison, of which McConnell had complained. Czupor also testified that Casey was the Committee's enforcer.

Paxman, a guard, testified that he felt something was wrong when he saw Casey going down to D Range. Because the other guard on duty had gone to the bathroom, Paxman was alone. He left the bubble, locked it, and went to the area at the top of the stairs from which he could see down the range. Another guard, James Schwab, testified that he was duty at Main Control and saw an incident in the breezeway involving May. He called the officer on duty in the bubble and warned him to be on the look-out. There was evidence that trouble was brewing and this was evidence from which a jury could conclude that the means had been assembled to carry out an attack on McConnell.

A prisoner, John Mills, was a defence witness. On a trip to the canteen with a friend he saw at least 8 or 9 inmates standing on either side of the breezeway around supper-time. Among the group was the Inmates Committee, including Marrigan, Childs and Pratt, all of whom would otherwise be running the canteen at that time. Pratt and Casey followed Mills and his friend towards the canteen and asked what they were doing, and if they had a problem. Mills responded that they were just going to the canteen and that they had no problem. On the way back from the canteen, Mills noticed the group was visibly armed with knives and appeared to be waiting for someone. The men were standing just outside of the camera range. Mills was asked for his impression on seeing the knives. He responded, in part, as follows:

Q When you see a bunch of guys with knives, that would suggest, would it, that something was happening?
A Something is going to happen when you see that.
Q Did you know or have any idea as to what was going to happen?
A I pretty well had an idea, yeah.
Q Tell us, please, what your ideas were, what you thought was going to happen and why?
A I heard when I was on earlier that these guys were going to take out some other inmates.
Q Okay. How did you get that, through gossip with your other inmates?
A It was gossip and I heard a threat.
Q You heard a threat?
A Yeah.
Q Who did you hear threaten who?
A That was one of the people I mentioned earlier (Marrigan, Childs, Pratt, etc.] threatened these guys here.
Q Which guys where?
A Excuse me?
Q Who? Okay. One of these guys that you mentioned earlier?
A Right. It is kind of hard because I don't want to bring in, because I have to walk a fine line here between having something happen to me.
Q I know what your difficulty is, Mr. Mills, but you say that you heard one of these people who were in that group make threats?
A Yeah.
Q Can you tell me who the threat was made to?
A I heard threats made to Lorne [McConnell] and there was threats made to May as well.
Q What else?
A The threats to May were just before supper.
Q That was before supper?
A Just before, just before we had to lock up.
Q Okay. So that was before you went out and saw these people on the breezeway?
A Yeah.
Q You had seen some of them and heard things said to McConnell? Okay. What sort of threat was it?
A The threat to May was he said that he didn't want to see anyone get hurt and the threat to him was, "You stay in the middle, you die in the middle."
Q That was to whom?
A That was to May because he didn't want to see anyone get hurt.
Q May was trying to be a peace maker.
A Yeah.
Q Was told, "If you stay in the middle, you will die in the middle?"
A Yeah.
Q Okay. So you had a pretty good idea by the time you got back to the range, to your unit, that somebody was going out that night?
A Yeah, someone was going to be getting it. It is hard to explain but you can, when you -- it is like you can walk outside and you can feel it. It's like you know something is going to happen. Everybody is all tensed up and you can just feel everybody, they are just acting a lot different than they usually do.
Q Would you say that is a result of living in the institution, you get this kind of extra instinct?
A Yeah. I have never felt it on the street being out. It is like you know, you know something is going to happen. It's like, it is really hard to explain.
(A.B. Vol. V p. 741-2) (emphasis added)

Mills was in the vicinity of the "D" range when the killing occurred, but he did not see what took place.

The Killing

In anticipation of an attack McConnell "beefed up" by placing magazines and a clipboard under his clothing for protection, putting on his winter jacket, and arming himself with knuckle dusters and a stick. McConnell had asked Letendre and Haffter, fellow inmates, if they would stand by him. They, too, beefed up. Letendre had possession of a shank or home-made knife.

When May had tried to intervene he was struck by Casey. Once May had been struck, Czupor told Casey to get out before the guard saw him, and Czupor and Kreba then decided to take Casey to Kreba's cell, allegedly to diffuse the situation. They headed down the range to Kreba's cell. McConnell was aware that May had been struck. He, Letendre and Haffter were standing at the top of the stairs leading to D Range. McConnell testified that when he saw Czupor, Casey and Kreba walking up the stairs past the bubble door he thought the attack was beginning. Instead, the three men did not go up toward the D Range when they entered Unit 10, but went towards Kreba's cell, away from McConnell. McConnell described the ensuing events in the following terms: (p. 14)

... I watched Casey go by me, and I was scared -- I was pretty scared at the time, because I thought it's coming down; it's all coming down. I was also a little bit angry that this guy would beat up a man who has one arm, could not defend himself, and was only looking to make peace between not just one, not for me, but for everybody. And when he walked past me and he hit the top of the stairs, I said I was going to go down and give him a few punches.
(A.B. Vol. IV p. 701) (emphasis added)

He struck Casey in the back of the head with the knuckle-dusters. Casey fell to the ground and McConnell continued to strike him in the head area. According to the Crown witness, Czupor, Letendre then "... went to his stomach with an object" which he believed was an ugly knife. Paxman had seen most of the incident and ordered McConnell, Letendre and Haffter to drop their weapons, which included brass knuckles, 2 shanks, and 2 pieces of wood crafted with protruding nails. From their clothing they removed a number of magazines, a clipboard, and weight-lifting body belts.

The pathologist's report showed that Casey had suffered superficial but severe head wounds inflicted by a heavy object such as knuckle dusters. He sustained 6 stab wounds; the 3 fat wounds being those which entered the heart, liver and lung. The injuries to Casey's head were not severe enough to cause death.

McConnell testified in his own defence. He said that he thought Marrigan, Pratt and Casey intended to kill him that day, and that he had no choice but to defend himself. When he saw Casey on the range, he decided to strike first. His evidence was that he was thinking that after Kevin (May) got punched out, that it had started, or that it was coming down.

He was examined as to what his alternatives were. He said he could stand and fight or he could check in. To check in at Drumheller he would have to go down to Building 3. In order to get to building 3 he would have to go through the breezeway. With regard to that, he said "There's no way I was going outside. Forget it."

The theory of the Crown was that McConnell, Letendre and Haffter acted as parties in the killing of Casey. Because they prepared themselves in anticipation of the confrontation, the stabbing was a planned and deliberate murder. The position of the defence was that Casey had made prior threats of violence and the appellants acted under a reasonable apprehension of death or grievous bodily harm constituting self-defence within s. 34(2) of the Code.

Dr. Weston, a psychiatrist, gave expert opinion evidence for the defence. He was qualified as an expert in the areas of the psychiatry of offenders ("inmates") and the culture of inmate population in long-term prisons. He testified that inmates have their own system of justice, which must be allowed to continue if the prison system itself is to survive.

Dr. Weston advised that an inmate whose life has been threatened may protect himself in the following ways: (1) by hiding in his cell and hoping for the best; (2) by applying for protective custody; (3) by committing a crime and being temporarily confined to "the hole"; or (4) using his friends to try and protect himself and taking the law into his own hands. Different consequences are attached to each course of action.

Dr. Weston further testified that the granting of protective custody depended upon the warden and that it is granted less frequently than in the past in federal penitentiaries. He also testified that getting protective custody or thrown in "the hole" can take some time. Inmates have to go before the warden's court, before being sent to solitary confinement and that may take 24 hours depending upon the offence and other circumstances. Under cross-examination, Dr. Weston stated that if an inmate struck an officer, a trip to the hole would quickly ensue.

When asked to draw an analogy between the situation of an inmate and the condition of a person in the larger society, Dr. Weston replied:

I think in the final stages of the battered wife syndrome that you see the situation a women knows she is going to be killed or kill. I think it is very similar to that. This is virtually a life or death situation. It's going to be me or have to take some action and I think that is the similarity to it.
(A.B. Vol. V p. 7723)

It was suggested to Dr. Weston in cross-examination that the final stage of the battered wife syndrome occurs when one person has assaulted one person over a length of time, usually a sufficient length of time. He replied:

No. There may have been multiple assaults in that often these women, as you know, have been in previous assaulted relationships. So it is a whole series but it is the one which is the end, yes. But alls I'm doing is like -- wouldn't compare it with the battered wife syndrome because we haven't got all the factors .... We don't know enough about that but those things may exist because think that if we looked at some of the things that are seen in the battered wife syndrome they are in the penitentiary population. I see young offenders now who I know are going to the penitentiary. There is nothing I can do about it. I cannot save them in any way whatsoever because their fate is already determined by the age of 15, 16. Alls I am saying is there is a similarity in the final thoughts of the battered wife, kill or be killed and I think that occurs. (A.B. Vol. V p. 776)

Dr. Weston had not worked with or interviewed any or all of McConnell, Letendre and Haffter.

Jury Questions

During deliberations, the jury sought further instructions from the trial judge on two occasions. These questions are set out here. After deliberating for four hours, the jury asked its first question.

First Question

"Request judge's definition of various charge definitions (e.g., self defence, etc.) -- copy of."
(A.B. Vol. V p.893)

Following this first request for further instruction, one of the jurors asked the trial judge a question in open court. The juror enquired:

"With regard to self-defence, I'm not really clear on the time frame. You said immediately."
(A.B. Vol. V p. 906)

Second Set of Questions

1. In order for self-defence to apply to the accused, did the threat to him or his life have to be immediate??
2. Does the long term ongoing threat (i.e. build-up of the days events) meet the requirements for self-defence (re 34-2A)
3. If the accused did not use all available options, can self-defence be used?
(A.B. Vol. VII, p. 1063)

Shortly after the reply to the answer to the last questions, the jury returned with its verdict.

Issue 1 - Did the trial judge err in his direction to the jury with respect to the objective test?

The defence argues that the trial judge erred by inviting the jury to apply a purely objective test in determining whether there was a reasonable apprehension of death or grievous bodily harm.

The applicable section for self-defence on the facts of this case is s. 34(2) which reads:

Every one who is unlawfully assaulted and who causes death or grievous bodily harm in repelling the assault is justified if

(a) he causes it under reasonable apprehension of death or grievous bodily harm from the violence with which the assault was originally made or with which the assailant pursues his purposes; and
(b) he believes, on reasonable grounds, that he cannot otherwise preserve himself from death or grievous bodily harm.

An assault is defined as follows in s. 265(1)(b):

he attempts or threatens, by an act or a gesture, to apply force to another person, if he has, or causes that other person to believe on reasonable grounds that he has, present ability to effect his purpose; or

In R. v. Pétel, [1994] 1 S.C.R. 3, 87 C.C.C. (3d) 97, 26 C.R. (4th) 145 (S.C.C.), Lamer C.J.C. enumerated the three constituent elements of a defence under s. 34(2) at p. 12 of S.C.R.:

(1) the existence of an unlawful assault on the accused;
(2) a reasonable apprehension of a risk of death or grievous bodily harm; and
(3) a reasonable belief by the accused that is not possible to preserve oneself from harm except by the infliction of death or grievous bodily harm on the assailant.

Lamer C.J.C. emphasized the importance of the accused's state of mind at the critical time and said this about the requirement of assault under s. 34(2) at 1 S.C.R. 13:

An honest but reasonable mistake as to the existence of an assault is therefore permitted. This is also how the Ontario Court of Appeal understood it in R. v. Nelson (1992) 71 C.C.C. (3d) 449, at p. 455. The existence of an assault must not be made a kind of prerequisite for the exercise of self-defence to be assessed without regard to the perception of the accused. This would amount to a sense of trying the victim before the accused. In a case involving self-defence, it is the accused's statement of mind that must be examined, and it is the accused (and not the victim) who must be given the benefit of the doubt. The question the jury must ask itself is therefore not was the accused unlawfully assaulted? but rather did the accused reasonably believe, in the circumstances, that she was being unlawfully assaulted? (emphasis added)

Thus, the jury had to took to McConnell and Letendre and what they reasonably believed in the circumstances. Their perception must be reasonable. The assault may be a threat to apply force if the assailant has, or causes the accused to believe on reasonable grounds that he has, present ability to carry out the threat.

The appellants' counsel argue that the trial judge erred in his description of the objective requirement of the test.

The trial judge stated, in part, the following:

In deciding whether he had reasonable grounds for this belief, you may consider what an ordinary, reasonable person would have believed in those circumstances. So, it is not what the accused himself had believed. It is what a reasonable person would have believed in those circumstances. Would a reasonable person in the circumstances of that day believed that Casey was going to kill him or cause grievous bodily harm and that the only means of escape was to attack first. (A.B. Vol. V, p. 866)

And later:

When considering this provision of self-defence, you are called upon to decide what perception the accused had as to the danger to himself and whether such a perception was based on reasonable grounds. A person may be acting in self-defence even if their perception of a situation is mistaken, provided that it is reasonably based. There may have been a mistake as to perception of the situation but was it reasonably based? The mistake must be one which an ordinary person using ordinary care would have made in the circumstances. In all the circumstances, would an ordinary person have made the mistake that the accused made in believing that they had to act in self-defence? (A.B. Vol. V, p. 866-7)

Again, in answering a question from the jury, the trial judge instructed the jury as follows:

Again in the circumstances here, are there reasonable grounds for him to believe or would a reasonable person, an ordinary person in these circumstances, believe that there is no other way to protect yourself than to kill the person who is attacking you? (A.B. Vol. V, p. 900)

And another:

And from that, to make that determination, you have to look at it as an ordinary, reasonable person would look at it. In all those circumstances, was it reasonable for him to do what he did? Did he have reasonable grounds to believe that he could not otherwise preserve himself from death or grievous bodily harm? (A.B. Vol. V, p. 907)

It is obvious from the second question that the jury was still having difficulty and asked for further instructions. During the second recharge, the trial judge said this:

The convicts' code require - - the prison code requires that these things be settled amongst themselves. That is not the law. The convicts' code does not replace the law of this country. The law of this country is that you must judge whether what was done was reasonable from the ordinary person's standpoint. (A.B. Vol. V, p. 917)

I agree with counsel that there is some confusion in these quotations. While a jury is entitled to test an accused's subjective state of mind by reference to an objective standard, care must be taken not to apply a purely objective test. The difficulty arises with respect to how objective the objective test must be. For instance, reasonableness must be assessed in light of the special circumstances of the accused (R. v. Lavallee, [1990] 1 S.C.R. 852). In Lavallee, supra, Wilson J. stated:

The definition of what is reasonable must be adapted to circumstances which are, by and large, foreign to the world inhabited by the hypothetical "reasonable man". (S.C.R. 874)

The Crown argues that from the whole of the charge, the jury would understand that it was the accused's perception that must be measured. If one considers the entire charge it is clear that it is the perception of the accused that is being measured. For instance, in describing the import of the prison environment, the trial judge stated with respect to s. 34(2)(a): In making that consideration, of course, you can take into account the general prison environment, but the general prison environment cannot replace the law of Canada. The law of the country must still prevail in a prison environment. So simply because there is a certain culture, a certain environment in the prison, does not exclude the operation of the law, including the law of self-defence. But if you take it down to the individual level, did these accused, or, the one of them with respect to whom you are considering as self defence, in the context of the environment in which he lived and in the context of the situation of that day, have reasonable grounds to believe that he was in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm at the hands of Casey? (A.B. Vol. V p. 865-6)

And again with respect to s. 34(2) Criminal Code belief he stated:

You must ask yourself whether the accused had this belief. If he did, then you must ask yourself whether, given the history, circumstances and perception of the accused, his belief that he could not preserve himself from death or serious bodily harm except by killing Casey was reasonable. (A.B. Vol. V p. 867)

Certainly the last instruction indicates that the trial judge did properly put the test to the jury. The Crown concedes that the learned trial judge spent more time dealing with the meaning of a "reasonable apprehension" of death or grievous bodily harm, than what might constitute "reasonable grounds" for believing that one could not otherwise preserve oneself, but argues that was only natural and appropriate, given the complexity of these notions. It is the Crown's position that from a reading of the whole of the charge it is clear that it is the accused's belief that was material.

The trial judge said:

... for this section to apply, for an accused to get the benefit of it, when that accused stabbed or struck the deceased Casey, he must have been afraid that Casey was going to kill him or was going to cause him serious harm. It is only in those circumstances that this defence will apply.

Now, your first task will be to determine whether at the time when Casey was stabbed and struck, whether the persons involved in that, or person involved in that, believed that he was in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm and whether that person had reasonable grounds for that belief. (A.B. Vol. V p. 865) (emphasis added)

There were many other portions of the charge where the trial judge carefully discussed the appellants' subjective beliefs. In my view, a reading of the whole of the charge would make it clear that it was the accused's reasonable subjective belief that must be considered, at least as it relates to apprehension. The trial judge's instruction with respect to the reasonableness of the accused's belief that he could not otherwise preserve himself from death or grievous bodily harm is more problematic. However, in view of my findings on his instructions on immediacy, it is unnecessary for me to deal with the retreat issue.

The "Imminent or Immediate" Issue

In my view, the substantive issue in this appeal relates to the instruction of the trial judge in reply to questions raised by the jury and the requirement of immediacy as part of the assault charge. In order for the defence provided by s. 34(2) to apply there must be an assault. The appellants argue that the trial judge erred in his charge and recharge by equating present ability with immediacy.

The trial judge explained what an assault was in his main charge to the jury. He asked them to consider whether there was a "present ability" for any threat which might have been made to McConnell to have been carried out. The relevant portion of his charge is excerpted below.

A person also commits assault, according to the law, when he attempts or threatens to apply force to another person, if that person who makes the attempt or threat has or causes the other person to believe on reasonable grounds that he has the present ability to carry out that purpose.

Let me give you an example. If I am standing face-to-face with Mr. Jones, and I raise my fist to him and do it in a threatening manner, that's a threat. And I'm close enough to Mr. Jones to effect my threat, I'm within striking distance, I can lash out and I can hit him, so that the mere fact of threatening in a situation where I can carry out my threat and where Mr. Jones believes that I can carry out my threat, that is an assault.

On the other hand, if I was standing over on the roof of that building out there and hollering at Mr. Jones and shaking my fist at him, that would not be an assault, because although the action is the same, I don't have the present ability. That is, at that moment I don't have the ability to carry out my threat. To get from there to here to hit Mr. Jones would take several minutes. Mr. Jones could easily be gone, and so I can't carry out my threat, so I don't have the present ability to carry out my threat.

So an assault can be a threat in circumstances where the person making the threat has the present, or immediate, ability to carry out the threat and --- or, the other person believes that I have the present or immediate ability to carry out the threat. So, you needn't consider, when you are considering self-defence and looking at the word "assault," that there must be an actual physical assault. An assault can be made out by a threat where there is a present ability to carry out the threat or the other party believes, on reasonable grounds, that the person making the threat has the immediate ability to carry out the threat.

That explanation of assault is important because one of the defences in this case is obviously self-defence. Mr. McConnell testified and Dr. Weston explained the culture within a prison, and McConnell testified that he had a confrontation earlier on October 21 with Pratt, and Pratt apparently backed down, and McConnell was told that this will be dealt with after dinner, after supper, "We'll settle this another way." There were some words of a threat, some words in the nature of a threat, and that could constitute an assault. It is a question for you to decide whether, in all the circumstances, those words in the context of the prison environment constituted an assault against McConnell, the threat with what McConnell says he saw happening - some of the people huddling, talking together, according to his evidence - whether he had the fear and reasonable ground for believing that the people who had threatened him had the present ability to carry out their threat. (A.B. Vol. V p. 862-3-4) (emphasis added)

The appellants claim the trial judge erred in his instructions to the jury on this issue.

And again in his main charge, the trial judge said this to the jury:

... for this section to apply, for an accused to get the benefit of it, when that accused stabbed or struck the deceased Casey, he must have been afraid that Casey was going to kill him or was going to cause him serious harm. It is only in those circumstances that this defence will apply.
...
Now, your first task will be to decide whether at the time when Casey was stabbed and struck, whether the persons involved in that, or person involved in that, believed that he was in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm and whether that person had reasonable grounds for that belief.

In making that consideration, of course, you can take into account the general prison environment, but the general prison environment cannot replace the law of Canada. The law of the country must still prevail in a prison environment. So simply because there is a certain culture, a certain environment in the prison, does not exclude the operation of the law, including the law of self-defence. But if you take it down to the individual level, did these accused, or, the one of them with respect to whom you are considering as self-defence, in the context of the environment in which he lived and in the context of the situation of that day, have reasonable grounds to believe that he was in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm at the hands of Casey? (A.B. Vol. V p. 865-6) (emphasis added)

In response to the jury's request for the trial "judge's definition of various charge definitions", the trial judge avoided using the words "imminent" or "immediate". He said only the following with respect to this issue:

Are there reasonable circumstances surrounding this event from which the accused can reasonably be afraid that he would be killed or that he would have grievous bodily harm caused to him because of the violence with which the assault was originally made, that is the threats, what were perceived to be threats by Pratt earlier in the day that "We'll settle this later tonight," the question there for you is: Is this a violent assault that Pratt made and was it reasonable under the circumstances for these accused or one or more of them to feel that they were being threatened with death or grievous bodily harm? (A.B. Vol. V p. 899)

After his response, the trial judge solicited other questions of the jury. One juror asked this question in open court:

Q. With regard to self-defence, I'm not really clear on the time frame. You said immediately. (A.B. Vol. V p. 906)

The trial judge responded to this question as follows:

Well, yes. You see, you have to look at the situation at that moment. You have to look at the situation at that moment. There's no question about that, but you have to take into account as well what is in the person's mind; that is, in McConnell's mind or Letendre's mind ...

With respect to Letendre, you know that he and McConnell talked in the afternoon. McConnell asked him to back him up, but I don't recall any evidence that there was any threat of any kind directed at Letendre. In those circumstances, you have to ask yourself whether there is a possibility even that Letendre may have thought that he was defending himself in some way if he assaulted Casey.

So you have to look at the very moment when these things are happening, but it has to also be done in considering the background, because the background helps to establish the state of mind of the person. So is there any background here that permits you to say, "Yeah, Letendre had reasonable apprehension of death or grievous bodily harm because of the violence with which he was assaulted"? That in my view would be stretching it, okay?

So let's deal, then, specifically with McConnell. McConnell is the one who had the confrontation with Pratt earlier in the day; and Pratt or someone else, Marrigan, during the course of the day, I think twice there was reference made to, "We'll settle this another way" or "We'll deal with this after supper." Does that threat in your view constitute a threat of death or grievous bodily harm? Was there a threat there to kill or do grievous bodily harm, and did it set the stage? Did it put McConnell's mind in such a state where he had reason to believe when he saw what was happening with Casey - Casey was walking away from him, walking towards Kreba's cell - did he have reason to believe that it was about to happen, that he was about to get taken out? Or is it more likely that he was annoyed - I think he said he was somewhat angry because Casey attacked a one-armed man - and this was retribution, not self-defence?

So McConnell's state of mind has to be determined by not only what happened at that very moment or in those few seconds but also what happened earlier in the day; and you have to say, "Is it reasonable for him to be in the state of mind that he was fearing death or grievous bodily harm because of the nature of the actions towards him?"
...
So while it's an immediate situation and you look at the immediate circumstances, you have to look also at the background to consider the state of mind of the individual. (A.B. Vol. V p. 906-7) (emphasis added)

The jury was obviously still grappling with this issue, as two of the three questions the jury subsequently asked pertained to it:

(1) In order for self-defence to apply to the accused, did the threat to him or his life have to be immediate??
(2) Does the long term or ongoing threat (i.e. buildup of day's events) meet the requirements for self-defence (re 34-2A)

The third question was:

(3) If the accused did not use all available options, can self-defence be used?
(A.B. Vol. VII p. 1063)

The trial judge responded to these questions as follows:

The first question is in order for self-defense to apply to the accused, do the threat to him or his life have to be immediate. The answer I think is quite simple and I had given it this morning, but in a two-and-a-half-hour charge can understand why you would have missed it. The accused must believe that he was in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm. That is the term I used this morning, "in imminent danger of death or serious bodily harm." The word imminent and the word immediate are almost synonymous, although there is a slight difference. Immediate has many other meanings than the one, the temporal meaning exact in time. Imminent has fewer choices of definition but both of them mean ready to take place. So the simple answer to your question is yes, the accused must believe that the threat to his life was immediate.

Your second question is does the long-term or ongoing threat, that is buildup of day's events meet the requirements for self-defence re 34(2)(a)? I can't give you quite as simple an answer to that because to determine if the accused thought that there was immediate or imminent threat to his life or his safety, that is, to determine whether he reasonably believed that there was an imminent threat of death or serious bodily harm, you have to consider all the circumstances including the threats if you find that there were threats made earlier or if the words that were said about some of the things later were a form of threat. You have to decide whether in all circumstances the accused had reason to believe that there was a threat to his life or to his bodily integrity. So the circumstances must be taken into account. And it is up to you to decide whether the accused had reason to believe that there was an immediate or imminent threat of death or grievous bodily harm to him from the victim Casey. (A.B. Vol. V p.916) (emphasis added)

After hearing this response the jury retired for 10 minutes and returned with their verdict.

Appellants Argument - An Error

Appellants' counsel states that the leading authorities have rejected the rule requiring that the apprehended danger be imminent. It is argued that the alleged rule, which does not appear anywhere in the Code, is only an assumption based on common sense. Thus, they say, the trial judge fell into error when he told the jury to consider whether the accused subjectively believed that the threat from the deceased was imminent. The appellants cite R. v. Lavallee, supra; R. v. Pétel supra, and R. v. W.(D.), [1991] 1 S.C.R. 742, (1991), 63 C.C.C. (3d) 397 (S.C.C.).

In Pétel, at p. 14-15 (S.C.R.), Lamer C.J.C. stated the following about a trial judge's responsibility to accurately answer questions posed by a jury:

The same is not true, however, of the answer given by the judge to the question put to him by the jury regarding the relevance of the threats immediately preceding the incident of July 21 and the threats prior to that date. The importance of adequately answering questions put by the jury should be borne in mind: R. v. W.(D.), [1991] 1 S.C.R. 742, at pp. 579-60. The question will generally relate to an important point in the jury's reasoning, so that any error the judge may make in answering it becomes all the more damaging. It is often necessary to repeat certain aspects of the main charge in order to place the specific question in a more general context. (emphasis added)

Further, in Pétel, Lamer C.J.C. stated the following about the "alleged rule" that apprehended danger must be imminent, at p. 13-14:

Moreover, Lavallee, supra, rejected the rule requiring that the apprehended danger be imminent. This alleged rule, which does not appear anywhere in the text of the Criminal Code, is in fact only a mere assumption based on common sense. As Wilson J. noted in Lavallee, this assumption undoubtedly derives from the paradigmatic case of self-defence, which is an altercation between two persons of equal strength. However, evidence may be presented (in particular expert evidence) to rebut this presumption of fact. There is thus no formal requirement that the danger be imminent. Imminence is only one of the factors which the jury should weigh in determining whether the accused had a reasonable apprehension of danger and a reasonable belief that she could not extricate herself otherwise than by killing the attacker. (emphasis added)

It is here that the similarity between the present case and the final stages of a battered wife syndrome may be relevant, in the sense that the assault may be made by a threat, where the person reasonably believes that the person making the threat has the present ability to carry out the threat, notwithstanding that the physical aspect of the assault is not imminent or immediate. As Wilson J. stated in Lavallee at p. 120: "The issue is not, however, what an outsider would have reasonably perceived but what the accused reasonably perceived, given her situation and experience." Thus, here the appellants could reasonably believe that the threats were made, and were continuing, by persons with the present ability to carry them out, although they were not going to carry out the threat at that immediate moment.

Respondent's Argument - No Error

Respondent's counsel argues that the trial judge incorporated into the meaning of the term "imminent" all the evidence from the time of the original threat to the time of the killing itself. He was clear to include the background of threats made earlier in the day and the state of mind of the accused persons.

In any event, the respondent argues that the trial judge did not err in referring to the word "imminent", because imminence is still a factor for the jury to consider In determining the nature of the accused's apprehension involved. According to Pétel, the respondent argues, it is only when there is unequal bargaining strength (i.e., battered wife syndrome) that the importance of imminence declines. In other words, there is a sliding scale of importance associated with imminence. As strength between the parties equalizes, imminence becomes a greater factor. Here, the respondent argues, there was no unequal bargaining power between the parties.

Dealing with the last argument first, it is in my view that the equality of the bargaining strength is best left for the jury. McConnell was, like the others, an inmate. It is true that he was not acting entirely alone, he may have had a second assistant in Haffter. McConnell and the others attacked a group of three men - but it appeared that McConnell and Letendre were very vulnerable to a much larger group. The threatening group started with three or four members of the Inmates Committee, and expanded to eight or nine inmates, who were armed with knives and standing on either side of the breezeway. There was evidence from which it could be inferred that the group had the power to recruit other members of the inmate population, some of whom may have wished to gain favour with the Inmates Committee. There was evidence of the flurry of activity McConnell witnessed in the courtyard between the Inmates Committee and others.

In my view, the jury's final questions were the pivotal questions. They indicated that the jury was totally aware of the main issue in this case. The jury had deliberated several hours when the last questions were asked. What they really wanted to know was whether the law permitted a preemptive strike, or whether the actual physical component of the threatened assault had to be immediate. The trial judge was careful on the subjective element, and the need to have regard for the circumstances of the case, but I am satisfied he may have taken away the very defence he tried to leave with the jury when he said that the accused must believe that the threat to his life was "immediate". There is a danger the jury would think that the possibility of a pre-emptive strike, or the battered wife syndrome type defence was not available. "Present ability" is not synonymous with "immediate".

I agree with the Crown that this trial judge communicated on many occasions that it is the subjective belief that must be measured. I also agree that the trial judge was correct in pointing out that the appellants' belief should be measured at the exact point of time they struck the victim. That, however, is different from saying that they must have apprehended that the physical component of the apprehended assault would have been carried out at that exact moment.

The jury's questions must be related to the facts of the case. On these facts, the three men who were part of the larger threatening group were walking away from the accused. It may be that they were going to get weapons with which to commit the assault they had threatened. The jury might have thought from the re-charge that the defence was not open until that point of time at which the attackers were moving towards him, notwithstanding their present ability to do the harm threatened. It is obvious if they were walking away, the assault was not going to occur at that time. The problem is that when the trial judge said: "So the simple answer to your question is yes, the accused must believe that the threat to his life was immediate," the jury may have thought it was not open for them to find self-defence because the appellants could not reasonably believe that the threats were going to be carried out at that moment. This would eliminate the possibility of a preemptive strike. The questions of the jury indicate that that was their very concern and, notwithstanding a careful charge, the requirement of immediacy as an element as opposed to a factor for consideration is, in my view, an error. While the appellants' state of mind must be measured at the exact point in time they struck Casey, they could be acting in self-defence to an assault by threat with present ability to carry out the threat, although the actual physical act threatened was not to be carried out at that exact moment.

I must also make reference to the respondent's argument that the battered wife syndrome arises only after a history of assaults, and that there was no such history in the case at bar. Obviously this fact situation is not identical to the battered wife syndrome, but analogous to it. It is, what I shall refer to as, "the prison environment syndrome". There was evidence from Dr. Weston about inmate behaviour and prison culture and the similarity in the environment to the battered wife syndrome. There is evidence about the environment being one in which inmates had to "kill or be killed". Thus a person could believe he or she was being assaulted (a threat with present ability) without it being immediate.

Moreover, while there is no history of assault against these particular appellants, it is not entirely accurate to say there is no history of assault in the case at bar. There was plenty of evidence that the deceased was the Committee's enforcer, that he collected on debts through force and intimidation. There was evidence that he had recently beaten the appellant's friend after the friend tried to intercede in the dispute. In summary, there was evidence in this case from which the jury could easily conclude that the strengths of the parties were unequal. Immediacy is only one factor to be considered in determining whether or not these appellants had, at the moment of striking, a reasonable belief that they were being assaulted.

In fact, Dr. Weston was cross-examined about the final stages of the battered wife syndrome, which are the culmination of a long string of assaults. He replied:

No. There may have been multiple assaults in that often these women, as you well know, have been in previous assaulted [sic] relationships. So it is a whole series but it is the one to one which is the end, yes. But alls I am doing is like -- I wouldn't compare it with the battered wife syndrome because we haven't got all the factors. We haven't got the childhood factors. We don't know enough about that but those things may exist because I think that if we looked at some of the things that are seen in the battered wife syndrome they are in the penitentiary population. I see young offenders now who I know are going to the penitentiary. There is nothing I can do about it. I cannot save them in any way whatsoever because their fate is already determined at the age of 15, 16. Alls I am saying is there is a similarity in the final thoughts of the battered wife, kill or be killed and I think that occurs. (A.B. Vol. V p. 776) (emphasis added)

As the jury returned the verdict only 7 to 8 minutes after the judge's answer to the jury's question about the required immediacy of the assault, I am of the view that the error was material.

That brings me to the last issue raised by Côté J.A. Are the facts here such that they cannot reasonably support a finding of assault on Letendre and McConnell which would justify self-defence, in any event? With respect, I disagree.

In my view there was some evidence from which a jury could conclude that an assault, as described in Pétel, was occurring. There was considerable evidence that a group of armed prisoners were gathering to carry out an assault on McConnell. It could be inferred from the facts that this assault would also be directed against any one who assisted him. There was evidence of the earlier verbal threat against McConnell and May, the Committee's refusal to make a truce, and McConnell's observation of the gathering of the inmates with weapons. There was evidence that Casey was part of that group, that he was the group's enforcer on certain matters and that Casey had already started the attack by striking May and telling him to stay out of it. There was evidence of Marrigan's statement that it was too late to retreat. Threats were communicated to McConnell directly and indirectly. He observed the gathering. He told Letendre, and there was evidence Letendre was with him. While Letendre did not give evidence, I cannot say the trial judge erred by leaving the defence for Letendre as well. There was evidence from which a jury could infer that the accused were subjectively of the belief, on reasonable grounds, that they were under threat of serious bodily injury by persons who had the present ability to carry out that threat. There certainly was evidence that the group had the means to carry out very serious, even fatal, injury to the accused. There was evidence that McConnell thought that the entry of Casey's group into Building D was the start of it. There was evidence from one guard that he had warned another guard in the "bubble" that trouble was brewing. Dr. Weston's evidence was also on point. He gave expert evidence on communication in the prison environment and the feeling of helplessness or the "kill or be killed" attitude that could prevail amongst the prisoners.

The trial judge felt there was a sufficient evidentiary basis to put the issues of assault, and consequently, self-defence to the jury. I would not interfere with that decision. In saying that, recognize the weakness of Letendre's situation, as did the trial judge. However, even if he did not testify, there was evidence from which the jury could conclude that anyone standing with McConnell would be assaulted, and could subjectively believe on reasonable grounds that he was being assaulted in the manner required by Pétel. The trial judge noted the weakness of Letendre's position and it may be that Letendre would more properly fit under defence of third persons, which was not raised until the appeal. However, it is my position that the earlier threats and the continuing and escalating activity with present ability to carry them out, could justify the pre-emptive strike, depending on the jury's findings.

On the whole of the evidence, the trial judge was right to leave the defence with the jury. In my view a new trial is required because I am concerned that the jury might have thought it was not open to the appellants to defend themselves unless the actual physical part of the assault was imminent or immediate.

In view of my finding, I will not deal with the arguments with respect to the issue of retreat.

In the result, I would order a new trial.

CONRAD J.A.
